ground_truth.title: How does residential electricity work?
ground_truth.author: user3080392
ground_truth.main_content:
I'm trying to understand how the electricity works in my house. I live in the US. I know that at the transformer, the primary winding induces a current in the secondary winding. The secondary winding is center tapped with a neutral line. The neutral line is grounded to the Earth. I have three questions: 1) When the circuit is closed, are hot 1 and neutral both hot? That is, are they both carrying a current of 120V that is alternating back and forth? 2) When the circuit is open, there is no current flowing. So, what are the electrons in hot 1 doing? Are they still oscillating back and forth? In other words, when a conductor is energized but no current is flowing through it, how are the electrons behaving? 3) When the circuit is open, the neutral is not energized like hot 1. Why not? I know it has to do with it being grounded to the Earth, but I have not found an explanation of what is happening at the level of the electron. Are the electrons alternating back and forth in and out of the Earth instead of through the neutral wire?

The accepted answer explains: First, a current of 120V doesn't make sense - volts are units of voltage, not current. The neutral wire carries current determined by the load, but its potential remains close to ground. The term hot refers to voltage potential with respect to ground, not current. When the circuit is open, if there is parasitic capacitance between the hot line and neutral/earth, electrons in the hot wire will flow back and forth charging and discharging this capacitive load. The neutral line cannot develop a potential relative to ground because if there were, a large current would flow and equalize the potentials. Another answer clarifies that hot is always 120V and neutral is always 0V regardless of whether the circuit is closed. The voltage exists independently of the current. When no current is flowing, ignoring secondary effects like capacitance, the electrons are taking a rest - they are not moving to and fro under the effect of an electric field as they would if the circuit was closed.
with: I'm trying to understand how the electricity works in my house. I live in the US.
The secondary winding is center tapped with a neutral line. The neutral line is grounded to the Earth.
When the circuit is closed, are hot 1 and neutral both hot? That is, are they both carrying a current of 120V that is alternating back and forth?
